planting.note: Plant container shrubs in late winter, spring, or fall in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Winter Gem requires protection from hot afternoon sun in zone 9 — partial shade is preferred and helps prevent the bronzing and bleaching that can affect boxwood foliage in intense summer heat.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in partial shade to full sun with afternoon shade protection; Winter Gem is the most widely planted Korean boxwood cultivar — a tight, globe-forming selection that maintains exceptional dense, rounded form with small, dark-green, lustrous leaves that hold their color better than many boxwood cultivars through winter and summer. The deep, rich green color with a slightly glossy sheen is particularly attractive as a formal edging or clipped topiary subject. Unlike many Buxus sempervirens cultivars, Winter Gem shows markedly superior resistance to boxwood blight (Calonectria pseudonaviculata), which has devastated English boxwood plantings across much of the Eastern United States — this disease resistance is a major reason Korean boxwood cultivars have increased in popularity. Winter Gem tolerates zone 9 summer heat better than English boxwood selections, provided afternoon shade protects from the most intense sun. Boxwoods require well-drained soil — they are extremely susceptible to Phytophthora root rot in wet, poorly drained sites. No serious pests affect boxwood in Gulf Coast gardens except the boxwood leafminer, which causes blistering of the leaves; treat with systemic insecticide if infestation is severe. Shear or hand-prune to maintain desired formal shape in late winter or after spring flush.

common_name: English Edging Boxwood (True Dwarf Boxwood, Dwarf English Boxwood)

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in late winter, spring, or fall in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in partial shade; Suffruticosa is highly susceptible to boxwood blight — do not plant near existing boxwood plantings where blight has been detected, and avoid planting in humid, poorly drained locations that favor disease.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in partial shade with some direct sun; Suffruticosa is the true dwarf English boxwood — the cultivar used for centuries in European formal knot gardens and low hedges, producing extremely dense, slow-growing, fine-textured, bright-green foliage mounds that clip beautifully into precise geometric forms. The very slow growth (6–9 inches per decade) and naturally tidy, globe-shaped habit make Suffruticosa the traditional edging boxwood for formal herb gardens, parterre gardens, and low border edging — a role it has filled in European gardens since at least the seventeenth century. The sweet, resinous fragrance of the foliage is the quintessential boxwood scent, associated with formal gardens throughout the Western garden tradition. However, Suffruticosa is highly susceptible to boxwood blight, the devastating fungal disease (Calonectria pseudonaviculata) that has been spreading across the Eastern United States since 2011 and can kill entire boxwood plantings within a season. For new plantings in areas where boxwood blight is present, disease-resistant Korean boxwood cultivars (Winter Gem, Green Velvet, Green Mountain) are strongly recommended over English boxwood. Where boxwood blight is not a current concern, Suffruticosa remains an outstanding traditional garden plant requiring precise siting in well-drained, aerated soil with good air circulation.

common_name: Hot Lips Salvia (Bicolor Jame Salvia, Red-and-White Sage)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Hot Lips requires excellent drainage and lean to average soil — in rich, moist soil it becomes floppy and short-lived.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Hot Lips is one of the most popular and widely planted ornamental salvias for Gulf Coast gardens — a Salvia × jamensis hybrid (a cross between S. greggii and S. microphylla) producing a striking and unusual bicolor flower pattern: each small tubular flower displays pure white lower lip with red upper lip, creating a vivid two-toned effect that gives the plant its theatrical name. The bicolor pattern is highly variable and temperature-dependent — in cool weather (fall, spring) the flowers display more white; in heat they shift toward more red; some flowers may be entirely red or entirely white. This variability is part of the cultivar's charm — the same plant displays different flower color proportions through the seasons. Hot Lips blooms continuously from spring through fall in zone 9, with the most intense flower production in the cooler months of spring and fall. Hummingbirds are strongly attracted to the red tubular flowers. The compact, mounding habit stays 18–30 inches and is virtually maintenance-free — light trimming after each bloom flush encourages fresh growth and continued flowering. Unlike larger shrubby salvias, Hot Lips is scaled for small gardens and container culture. Extraordinarily drought-tolerant once established; excellent in rock gardens and full-sun mixed borders.

planting.note: Plant container trees in spring after danger of frost has passed in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.0–7.0), fertile soil in full sun; Clementines are slightly more cold-hardy than navel oranges but should still be protected from temperatures below 28°F.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.0–7.0), fertile soil in full sun; Clementine is the most widely grown and beloved easy-peel mandarin orange in the world — the source of the small, bright-orange, seedless (when grown without cross-pollination) tangerines marketed as 'Cuties' and 'Halos' in American grocery stores. The fruit is small (2–2.5 inches diameter), very easy to peel with virtually no pith, with 8–14 segments of rich, sweet, complex mandarin flavor and distinctive floral aroma. The thin, glossy, bright-orange rind peels effortlessly, making Clementines the most practical fresh-eating citrus for children and adults who want convenient, mess-free citrus. Fruit ripens in November through January in zone 9 — the holiday season — making it one of the most seasonal and eagerly anticipated citrus fruits. The tree is compact, well-shaped, and attractive, with glossy dark-green foliage and fragrant white spring flowers. Clementines can produce seeds if cross-pollinated by other mandarin or sweet orange varieties growing nearby — for seedless fruit, avoid planting near other mandarins, or select a seedless clementine variety. In zone 9, Clementines should be sited in warm, protected locations and protected from temperatures below 28°F. Apply citrus fertilizer three times annually: February, May, and September.

planting.note: Plant container trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.0–7.0), fertile soil in full sun; Moro blood orange requires cool nights (45–55°F) to develop its characteristic red pigment (anthocyanins) — in areas with warm winters the flesh color may be reduced or absent.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.0–7.0), fertile soil in full sun; Moro is the deepest-colored blood orange cultivar — a Sicilian selection producing dramatic, dark crimson to near-black flesh with a striking red-streaked rind exterior. The flavor is distinctly more complex than standard sweet oranges, with a pronounced berry-like, raspberry-blackberry tone layered over the sweet orange base — a flavor unlike any other citrus. The deep red color comes from water-soluble anthocyanin pigments that develop only when cool nights (below 55°F) occur during fruit development; in very warm winters the flesh may show less red coloration but still produces the characteristic flavor. In zone 9, Moro blood oranges ripen in December through February — the coolest months — when the anthocyanin development is most favorable. The small to medium, round to slightly oval fruits have a thin, smooth rind. Blood oranges are highly valued in cooking for their dramatic color in fresh-squeezed juice (which turns deep ruby-red to purple), salad dressings, sauces, and cocktails. The juice color provides a culinary application that no other citrus can replicate. Protect from temperatures below 28°F; site in thermally protected areas of zone 9 gardens.
